items.title: Bacteria discovered with the ability to jettison cells as a survival mechanism
items.summary: Popular science fiction is no stranger to escape-pod scenarios, typically featuring characters who narrowly avoid their demise by jettisoning from a spaceship—think R2-D2 and C-3PO shooting away from a rebel spaceship in the opening of Star Wars: A New Hope. items.title: How proximity steals energy from nanoresonators
items.summary: Nanomechanical resonators are miniature vibrating structures on chips that oscillate at frequencies ranging from a few kilohertz to gigahertz. They are used as ultrasensitive detectors of mass and force, temperature and pressure, and as components in radio frequency filters and on-chip clocks. Moder

items.title: Raptorial insect forelegs evolved repeatedly but never converged on one winning design
items.summary: The evolutionary paths that created snatching forelimbs in insects multiple times moved in a similar direction but didn't end at a single solution. Kobe University research is pioneering a study of how organs with similar functions evolve, providing a new analytical approach to identify evoluti

items.title: Ancient rocks reveal how water reshaped Earth's interior 3.1 billion years ago
items.summary: Geologists studying some of the planet's oldest volcanic rocks have uncovered new evidence that water was playing a major role in shaping Earth's interior and driving volcanic activity more than 3 billion years ago.

items.title: Death toll from China storms rises to 15, hundreds injured
items.summary: The death toll from devastating storms in parts of China rose to 15 on Tuesday, with hundreds more injured and tens of thousands evacuated, state media reported, as President Xi Jinping urged "all-out" rescue efforts.

items.title: Making scents of cannabis: How cannabis cultivar and preparation methods influence aroma
items.summary: Ongoing research reveals how the aroma of cannabis is influenced not only by the chosen cultivar, but also by different drying and storage methods. Through a combination of high-resolution chromatography and human-perception aroma assessments, this project provides detailed insights into the impact

items.title: Kineska sonda snimila prvu fotografiju Zemljinog "mini-mjeseca"
items.summary: KINESKA sonda Tianwen-2 stigla je do Zemljinog 'mini-mjeseca', asteroida Kamoʻoalewa, i poslala prvu snimku izbliza. Misija će prikupiti uzorke kako bi se otkrilo njegovo podrijetlo.

items.title: AI just supercharged the race to find room temperature superconductors
items.summary: Scientists have combined machine learning with quantum physics to discover two new superconductors and create a much faster way to search for many more. The technique could bring researchers significantly closer to the long-sought goal of a room-temperature superconductor.

items.title: The World Cup From 250 Miles Up
items.summary: Over the years, astronauts aboard the International Space Station have photographed several of the cities hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

items.title: Newly discovered mineral named for researcher Tyler Spano
items.summary: Tyler Spano's impact on the field of mineralogy is anything but small. So when a newly discovered mineral, modest in size but significant in meaning, was named spanoite in her honor, it became a fitting tribute to her contributions to the field.

items.title: Q&A: Harnessing climate investments to serve development goals
items.summary: Climate pledges are designed to help countries reduce emissions and adapt to a changing climate, but the current iterations of these pledges place additional stresses on vulnerable nations, an international team of researchers co-led by University of Michigan Engineering and KTH Royal Institute of T

items.summary: Last year, an interstellar traveler entered our solar system. items.title: Efficient method for preparing pseudopregnant mice promotes laboratory animal welfare
items.summary: In biomedical research using mouse models, preparing pseudopregnant recipient females is a standard step in embryo transfer protocols. items.title: What everyone gets wrong about the modern job search—and what actually works
items.summary: Job searching has never been more accessible—or more confusing. Platforms like LinkedIn, Indeed and employer career pages let candidates submit applications with just a few clicks. What happens after they click "submit," however, has become fertile ground for misinformation.

items.title: AI system translates protein sequences into text, helping reveal functions of unknown proteins

items.title: Newly identified marine bacterial enzyme opens new route to building bioactive compound libraries
items.summary: Many important medicines and agricultural compounds have origins in natural products made by microorganisms. items.title: Soil thickness controls landslide occurrence, study finds
items.summary: Researchers at University of Tsukuba analyzed high-resolution topographic data from airborne LiDAR to examine the relationships among landslide area, depth, and slope gradient.

items.title: Why customers stick with troubled brands: Emotional attachment may override perceived risk
items.summary: Strong emotional attachment to a brand may help explain why some consumers remain loyal even in the wake of negative publicity, according to research published in the International Journal of Business and Emerging Markets.

items.title: Wasted pumpkin peel can keep your food fresh
items.summary: Researchers at Kyushu University have developed a new food preservation solution. Using pumpkin peel as a raw material, they synthesized a nanomaterial for food packaging that slows the deterioration of fruit and other produce while reducing transport damage. The findings were published April 30, 20

items.title: Quantum computing: Laser-optical system offers full control over 2,000 trapped Rydberg atoms
items.summary: Fraunhofer ILT in Aachen has developed a highly complex laser-optical system for a quantum computer currently under construction at the 5th Institute of Physics at the University of Stuttgart. This system enables 2,000 Rydberg atoms to be positioned with submicrometer precision in the computer'

items.title: Cultural power distance limits classroom voices across three communication pathways, review finds
items.summary: A systematic review of research on intercultural communication in higher education finds that cultural "power distance," which is the extent to which people accept unequal authority, continues to shape who speaks, who is heard and how knowledge is shared in multicultural classrooms.

items.title: How to create the blackest black ever known: From nature to nanotechnology
items.summary: Achieving the blackest of blacks has been one of humanity's enduring challenges. It is a frontier that unites modern nanotechnologists with nature's ancient color palette.

items.title: Stored water can develop slimy bacterial films: Nanotechnology may be a solution
items.summary: When drinking water is stored in containers, a thin layer of microorganisms can grow at the interface of the container and water. This thin layer is called a biofilm. items.title: Raft of targeted conservation efforts may help boost leopard numbers in sub-Saharan Africa, study suggests
items.summary: There could be fewer leopards living across sub-Saharan Africa than previously believed, according to a new study that has analyzed suitable habitat and conditions. It is hoped that the research, led by conservation scientists at Nottingham Trent University, could be used to help plan vital recovery

items.title: Hummingbirds speed up pineapple family's evolution
items.summary: Hummingbirds make bromeliad plants split into new species twice as fast as other pollinators do, scientists at the University of Reading have found. The research team gathered records of which animals pollinate 403 types of bromeliad, which include pineapples and more than 3,700 species, and found t

items.title: Heat waves: Expert tips on redesigning UK homes to cope with hotter temperatures
items.summary: As the UK prepares for the third heat wave of 2026, most people will be hoping to keep cool at home. Building regulations to protect homes from overheating were introduced in 2022. items.title: Climate change means an earlier spring, which can be disorienting and threatening for migrating birds
items.summary: Spring migration has taken flight, but with rising temperatures and shifting seasons, birds are adjusting when and how they migrate to keep up with a rapidly warming climate. items.summary: Social media algorithms are increasingly revealing users' sexual orientation or gender identity before they have consciously come out to themselves or others, according to new research. items.title: Engineers discover 'unexpected motion' in drug-delivery robots
items.summary: One day, tiny swimming robots may travel through the human body to deliver drugs. The medication would target only areas of need—chemotherapy drugs for a tumor, for example—avoiding healthy tissue and minimizing side effects. items.title: What's the best way to support perfectionistic students? Teachers don't always agree
items.summary: Imagine a student spending hours meticulously erasing and rewriting a paragraph that is already excellent.

items.summary: The marine microalgae responsible for the most devastating effects of the South Australian harmful algal bloom (HAB) has now been shown to be the most toxic species of its kind ever studied.

items.title: Fighting the world's deadliest infection with PAC-MAN and AI
items.summary: Tuberculosis, caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), is the world's deadliest single-agent infection, responsible for 1.23 million deaths in 2024, according to the World Health Organization. items.summary: With new technologies come new opportunities. items.title: Record fireworks display choked Washington in toxic smoke
items.summary: The U.S. capital, Washington, saw its worst air quality of the year over the July 4 weekend, as smoke from a massive fireworks display sent pollution soaring to levels considered very unhealthy for the entire public, data showed Monday.

items.summary: Next-generation batteries that use new electrolyte materials could achieve far higher energy density than today's lithium-ion batteries, without many of the safety concerns. items.title: Preparing birds for life in the wild: New technique boosts survival after release
items.summary: Every year, thousands of parrots confiscated from the illegal wildlife trade arrive at rehabilitation centers across Latin America. For many of these birds, rescue centers can provide safety but not necessarily a path back to the wild.

items.title: Canadian Artemis II crew member to retire from space agency
items.summary: The Canadian astronaut who was part of the historic Artemis II crew that successfully voyaged around the moon earlier this year is retiring from the nation's space agency, officials said Monday.

items.title: Survival comes at a price: Frog study links salt adaptation to increased risk of disease
items.summary: Over generations, a small North American frog has learned how to survive in a world that's getting increasingly saltier. But new research from the University of Missouri suggests that adaptation comes with an unexpected trade-off.

items.title: Flash flood warnings issued for parts of New York City and Northeast as heat wave breaks
items.summary: Heavy rain and flooding are breaking a heat wave that gripped New York City and much of the Northeast last week.

items.title: Study examines resilience training for children who stutter
items.summary: A new University of Mississippi-led study suggests that a telepractice resilience program may help children who stutter develop coping and self-advocacy skills.

items.title: Detecting neutron sources by borrowing inference tools from cosmology

items.summary: A new analysis sheds light on the most likely route for the Carthaginian general's famous crossing of the Alps. items.title: Cave finds reveal modern humans and Neanderthals may have shared long-term cultural continuity
items.summary: Tens of thousands of years ago, Homo sapiens coexisted with Neanderthals, Homo neanderthalensis. items.title: Physics-informed AI could accelerate development of controlled-release drug patches, bandages
items.summary: Brown University researchers have developed a new artificial intelligence method for predicting the rate at which materials used in controlled drug-release systems release therapeutic agents. The new method could slash the development time for new therapeutic patches, bandages and implants.

items.title: Bees reveal emotion-like reactions, from 'lip licking' to head shaking, in new videos
items.summary: New research proving bumblebees exhibit emotion-like behaviors—previously thought to exist only in mammalian species—has implications for how scientists understand the consciousness of insects.

items.title: AI could hurt employers in race for top talent
items.summary: Companies embracing AI to recruit faster could be damaging their ability to compete for top talent, according to a major new study.

items.title: Neighbors shape plant life more than expected, scientists find
items.summary: Scientists at Leiden University have discovered that plants are strongly influenced by their neighbors, not just above ground but also through hidden networks in the soil. Their findings challenge long-held ideas about how plants shape their environment and could help improve sustainable farming.

items.title: Algorithm determines which firms have advanced capabilities—and what others must build first
items.summary: Understanding how firms build their capabilities, such as managerial skills, technology adoption and innovation capacity, is important for explaining why some firms perform better and how others can improve. items.title: School-based program to reduce loneliness and improve help-seeking among adolescents
items.summary: Adolescents experiencing social isolation or loneliness are at increased risk of developing mental health problems, highlighting the need for preventive interventions before these challenges worsen. items.title: Four new groups of indigenous cacao varieties discovered in Peru
items.summary: A new genetic analysis of hundreds of cacao trees representing traditional Amazonian varieties grown on farms across Peru has revealed four previously unidentified, genetically distinct groups. items.title: Unexpected discovery yields new graphene oxide production method
items.summary: Researchers in the Texas A&M University J. Mike Walker '66 Department of Mechanical Engineering have developed a new method for producing graphene oxide, a high-value carbon nanomaterial used in batteries, electronics and advanced manufacturing.

items.title: What do people really eat? New global database gives best answer yet
items.summary: A major new resource that provides one of the most comprehensive pictures yet of what people are eating around the world has been introduced in a new study by a UCL and University of Oxford researcher.

items.title: Alpine butterflies track warming uphill, but habitat loss may pose bigger risk
items.summary: A new study published in the journal Alpine Entomology has found that alpine butterflies in the Swiss National Park are closely matching the pace of local warming in their range shift to higher elevations.

items.title: Early parenting shapes the brain and socio-sexual behavior, rodent study shows
items.summary: Past psychology studies have consistently highlighted the importance of social bonds for survival, showing that enduring relationships are linked with a longer life expectancy, a more resilient immune system, better cardiovascular health and a lower risk of psychiatric conditions. items.title: Hidden fungal networks deliver carbon to green plants, experiment confirms
items.summary: Beneath forests, grasslands and wetlands lies a hidden underground network of fungi known as mycorrhizal networks, sometimes nicknamed the "wood-wide web." items.summary: The summers of 2021 and 2022 were tough seasons for Colorado's Blue Mesa Reservoir. items.title: Discrepancies in AI lunar crater catalogs discovered
items.summary: A new Southwest Research Institute-led study compared eight AI-generated lunar crater catalogs, discovering that many of their published performance metrics drop sharply when the databases are evaluated using the same scientific standards humans are held to. items.title: Childhood trauma may erode adult relationships through daily communication struggles
items.summary: Traumatic events from your childhood could have a lingering impact on your adult relationships, according to new research from the University of Georgia.

items.title: Universities must rethink how they prepare students for an AI-powered world, study argues
items.summary: Universities need to rethink how they teach, assess and prepare students for employment as artificial intelligence becomes an increasingly important part of everyday life and work, according to a new study from the University of Manchester. items.title: Why Antarctica froze millions of years before the Arctic
items.summary: East Antarctica hosts the largest ice sheet on Earth, containing enough water to raise global sea levels by 52 meters (171 feet) were it to fully melt. Yet scientists have been puzzled for decades about how and why this ice sheet formed.

items.summary: In the course of studying planets beyond our solar system (6,316 confirmed exoplanets and counting), scientists have discovered some very interesting systems. items.title: Nanobubbles cleaned up the Lincoln reflecting pool: Here's how they could be used on dying seas and lakes
items.summary: Ahead of the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence in the U.S., an ozone nanobubble system has been used to keep the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool clear. items.title: New approach boosts microplastic removal from wastewater
items.summary: RMIT University researchers have tested a more effective way to capture microplastics from wastewater, using a combination of microbubbles and nanobubbles to achieve removal rates of more than 90%.

items.summary: On Monday, NASA released a draft Request for Proposals (RFP) seeking feedback from American companies on the next phase of its commercial space stations strategy, aimed at ensuring a seamless transition of activities in low Earth orbit from the International Space Station.  “NASA’s review refle

items.title: A Super El Niño is coming: 5 hard‑won lessons the world can learn from Africa
items.summary: Climate prediction scientists announced in June 2026 that El Niño, a cycle that happens every two to seven years, had formed. It was expected to develop into one of the strongest on record—a "super" El Niño.

items.title: Larger brain, smaller face: Human evolution took a different course than previously thought
items.summary: A new study, published July 6, 2026, in the journal Nature Communications, suggests that two of the best-known trends in human evolution—brain growth and the reduction in the size of the face and jaw—may be far less attributable to directed natural selection than scientists have long assumed. items.title: Japan releases snowman-like asteroid image after flyby
items.summary: Rare images taken by a Japanese space probe during a flyby of a near-Earth asteroid have revealed that the space rock resembled a snowman, scientists said Monday.

items.summary: As NASA prepares for a sustained human presence on the Moon, missions will increasingly require spacecraft that can navigate and communicate without a direct connection to Earth. items.title: Moving forests to save them: Here are the risks and rewards of assisted tree migration
items.summary: Climate change is shifting forest conditions faster than local tree species can adapt. Higher temperatures, more frequent droughts and less predictable winters can weaken species that have been established in a region for centuries. In light of this, one idea is gaining ground: assisted migration.

items.title: Q&A: How camera-equipped homing pigeons could improve robotic vision in flight
items.summary: Contrary to common assumptions, pigeons do not lock their eyes in place during flight. Instead, they make slow, subtle eye movements that may help them gather more information about their surroundings.

items.title: We assume students see pictures in their minds as they learn. But not everyone can
items.summary: Picture a bright red apple. Most people can do this easily. They imagine the apple's shape, color and shine. But for others, the image is vague and blurry or they "see" nothing at all. This is known as aphantasia—a "blind mind's eye."

items.title: Scientists discover smart way to supercharge soft robotics and better support rehabilitation patients
items.summary: Researchers have found an ingenious way to make soft robots and wearable technology more than three times more powerful by harnessing the surface tension of a tiny liquid metal droplet smaller than a raindrop.

items.title: Upside‑down whales aren't sick or hurt—they're just resting

items.title: The evolutionary pressure behind sexual asymmetry revealed in yeast cell study
items.summary: A major transition in evolution is the shift from asexual to sexual reproduction in early organisms. But why would a yeast cell, which usually reproduces asexually, choose to mate with a very different partner in times of stress?

items.title: A last dance before death: Binary stars and the origins of interacting supernovae

items.title: Climate change is silencing the Pilbara barking gecko
items.summary: New research from Monash University has provided the first comprehensive assessment of the Pilbara barking gecko (Underwoodisaurus seorsus) and revealed the species is facing a heightened risk of extinction under a rapidly warming climate.

items.summary: Researchers at Tohoku University have discovered a promising strategy that converts harmful carbon dioxide into valuable fuels and chemicals by precisely altering nanoclusters made of copper.

items.summary: Food scientists at the National University of Singapore (NUS) have used machine learning to sift through more than 2,300 scent molecules from aromatic plants and identify compounds with sleep-promoting potential, an approach that could accelerate the discovery of natural sleep aids.

items.title: Phylogenomics reveals angel insects' ancestry, resolving century‑old 'Zoraptera problem'
items.summary: Zoraptera, also known as angel insects or ground lice, are tiny termite-like insects generally found underneath bark or in decaying wood. The Zoraptera group includes a few dozen known insect species that closely resemble each other.

items.title: Scholars examine how faculty define rigor in online college courses
items.summary: A new article by scholars Jennifer L. James, Ph.D., Karen Myers, DNP, and Olivia Miller, M.A., in the Journal of Educators Online, titled "Studying Faculty Perceptions of Rigor in Online College Courses: Compromising or Accommodating?" examines how faculty perceive academic rigor when teaching, grad

items.title: Study reveals social enterprise well-being pressures
items.summary: Social enterprises help communities thrive, but founder well-being is critical to sustaining their impact, UC research finds. Social enterprises create jobs, encourage ethical consumption, and address social and environmental challenges.

items.title: Did Israel's 'basket' initiative lower grocery bills?
items.summary: Researchers from the Coller School of Management at Tel Aviv University examined the Ministry of Economy's flagship "Israel's Basket" initiative and found that although it substantially reduced the prices of the 100 products included in the program, these reductions were accompanied by pri

items.title: Millions may be getting the wrong cholesterol test
items.summary: A new study suggests that apoB, a blood test that measures harmful cholesterol particles, is better than standard LDL cholesterol testing for deciding who needs more intensive treatment. Researchers found it could prevent more heart attacks and strokes while remaining cost-effective for the U.S. hea
